coredata.dc:title: Intense enhancement of yellow luminescent carbon dots coupled with gold nanoparticles toward white LED
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yijun
Zhang, Jiaqi
Zhang, Junwen
Lin, Sai
Huang, Yunyun
Yuan, Rongrong
Liang, Xiaojuan
Xiang, Weidong
coredata.dc:description: A novel gold-carbon dots (GCDs) exhibiting relatively high luminescence have been successfully synthesized by a facile solution cast method. Intriguingly, colloidal Au nanoparticles (Au NPs) present an enhancement effect on the photoluminescence of CDs, which can be attributed to the metal enhanced fluorescence (MEF) included two cooperative mechanisms and near-field interactions. Significantly, the 1.5–3 fold enhancement was obtained in the vicinity of Au NPs. The formation mechanism and chemical structure of the as-prepared GCDs were verified by a series of characterization techniques, such as high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The GCDs with optimal fluorescence was acquired by varying volume ratio of colloidal Au NPs to CDs, rendering bright visible light under 460 nm excitation and high quantum yield (QY) of 39%. Given that, a simple spin coating technology was developed for fabricating GCDs film composed of GCDs solution and quartz glass. Subsequently, GCDs-based white light emitting diodes (white LED) was designed through integrating blue LED chips with the yellow light-emitting GCDs film. In addition, the GCDs film at appropriate thickness possesses excellent optical performances with the corresponding optimized color coordinate (CIE) of (0.33, 0.36), the color rendering index (CRI) of 78.2, correlated color temperature (CCT) of 5653 K, and the luminous efficiency (LE) approaching to 43.75 lm W−1. These superior results demonstrate that GCDs constitute a versatile and effective route for achieving high-performance white LED.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Au nanoparticles
GCDs film
Fluorescence enhancement
White LED

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the colloidal Au NPs
Characterization of Au NPs, CDs and GCDs
PL proprieties and mechanisms of the GCDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of synthesis for the construction of GCDs. The GCDs 1, GCDs 2 and GCDs 3 are denoted as S1, S2 and S3, respectively.
XRD patterns of the Au NPs. The inset shows that of CDs.
TEM images of (a) Au NPs, (b) CDs and (c) GCDs and the corresponding HRTEM images (d)–(f) as well as size distribution images (g)–(i).

UV–vis absorption spectra of (a) the GCDs, CDs and the Au colloid (top right corner). The insets show the digital photographs of the solutions of Au NPs, the corresponding CDs and GCDs (left bottom). (b) the amplified region of the UV–vis spectra of GCDs from 360 to 530 nm.
Infrared spectra of AEAPMS, CDs, GCDs.
XPS spectra for the as-obtained CDs (a) XPS survey spectrum. (b) C1s region scan spectrum. (c) O1s region scan spectrum and (d) Si2p region scan spectrum.
XPS spectra for the as-obtained GCDs (a) Au 4f region scan spectrum. (b) C1s region scan spectrum. (c) O1s region scan spectrum and (d) Si2p region scan spectrum.

(a) PL emission spectra of the CDs and GCDs solution under 460 nm excitation. (b) The enhancement factors is corresponding to intensity of GCDs divided by intensity of CDs. (c) PL emission spectra of GCDs (S2) solution under different wavelengths recorded from 420 nm to 520 nm in 20 nm increments. (d) Graphical representation of current interpretation for MEF on gold nanostructure.
Fluorescence decay curves for CDs and GCDs.

(a) CIE color coordinates of the white LED fabricated by integrating a the 460 nm blue chip with different thickness GCDs 2 films (S1, S2, S3 and S4) and CDs (S5) with the same thickness with S3. (b) EL of S3. (c) The fabrication of the white LED based on the combination of GaN chips. (d) The photograph of the white LED based on GCDs 2 film with a blue LED chip. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Biexponential Fit values and QYs of CDs and CDs with different Fractions of Au NPs.
Photoelectric Parameters of White LED: S1S4 is the GCDs 2 Hybrids Films and S5 is the CD Film.

head.ce:title: Intense enhancement of yellow luminescent carbon dots coupled with gold nanoparticles toward white LED

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Wenzhou University, Wenzhou 325035, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Wenzhou University, Wenzhou, Zhejiang 325035, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel gold-carbon dots (GCDs) exhibiting relatively high luminescence have been successfully synthesized by a facile solution cast method. Intriguingly, colloidal Au nanoparticles (Au NPs) present an enhancement effect on the photoluminescence of CDs, which can be attributed to the metal enhanced fluorescence (MEF) included two cooperative mechanisms and near-field interactions. Significantly, the 1.5–3 fold enhancement was obtained in the vicinity of Au NPs. The formation mechanism and chemical structure of the as-prepared GCDs were verified by a series of characterization techniques, such as high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The GCDs with optimal fluorescence was acquired by varying volume ratio of colloidal Au NPs to CDs, rendering bright visible light under 460 nm excitation and high quantum yield (QY) of 39%. Given that, a simple spin coating technology was developed for fabricating GCDs film composed of GCDs solution and quartz glass. Subsequently, GCDs-based white light emitting diodes (white LED) was designed through integrating blue LED chips with the yellow light-emitting GCDs film. In addition, the GCDs film at appropriate thickness possesses excellent optical performances with the corresponding optimized color coordinate (CIE) of (0.33, 0.36), the color rendering index (CRI) of 78.2, correlated color temperature (CCT) of 5653 K, and the luminous efficiency (LE) approaching to 43.75 lm W−1. These superior results demonstrate that GCDs constitute a versatile and effective route for achieving high-performance white LED.

ce:section.ce:para: Compared to traditional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) and organic dyes, fluorescent Carbon dots (CDs) are superior in terms of high aqueous solubility, robust chemical inertness, stable fluorescence intensity and so on. So far, tremendous efforts have been made to exploit the potential applications of fluorescent CDs in bioimaging, pollutant detection and catalysis [1–6]. Recently, research showed that unique properties of low-cost and environment-friendly provide more possibilities for the utility of CDs in white light emitting diodes (white LED) [7–11]. Liu et al. reported electroluminescence of white LED based on CDs with a maximum external QY of 0.083% in 2011 [7]. Rogach et al. have achieved full-color emission tunability in a flexible CDs ionogel and the corresponding CIE of (0.24, 0.36) in white LED [11]. Chen's group fabricated colour-conversion white LED through combining LED chips with CD phosphors and CD-PMMA films [8,9]. Notablely, most of indexed synthesis methods require rigorous equipment, multi-step procedures, complicated and timeconsuming purification, or the obtained products usually possess dull short wavelength under single-wavelength ultraviolet (UV) excitation and the low quantum yield (QY), which have impeded considerably the practical application of CDs in white LED [6–9]. [12–14] Thus, fluorescence enhancement of the yellow color CDs and the high QY are quite desirable. Meanwhile, noble metal nanoparticles (e.g. Au and Ag) have attracted considerable attention because of their enhanced optical properties in Raman spectroscopy and photoluminescence (PL) for surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [15–17]. SPR, the oscillation of high-concentration free electrons, is generated by the stimulation of irradiated light at the surface of metal nanoparticles [18]. In addition, the theory of metal enhanced fluorescence (MEF) has been reported [19–21]. The interaction between the surface plasmon of metallic surfaces and fluorophores can greatly affect the PL intensity of fluorophores. Amalgamation of CDs with metal nanoparticles is providing some exceptional features in terms of their catalytic, electronic or photovoltaic properties [22–24]. Remarkably, Au nanoparticles (NPs) are preferred to promote the PL for their stability, antioxidation and easily controlled in contrast with that of the other metal NPs [25]. Moreover, Tim Muster et al. realized the PL enhancement of CDs by conjugating Au NPs and CDs to dendrimers (PAMAM) through an amidation reaction, which has not been exploited in white LED yet [26]. Liu et al. had successfully composed gold-carbon dots (GCDs) and applied them to dual fluorescent imaging for breast cancer cells [27]. However, the study on the metal-induced PL enhancement of CDs is far insufficient. To the best of our knowledge, rare work on GCDs-based LED have been reported yet.

Herein, the sensitized GCDs were successfully prepared by incorporating the wine red Au colloid into the yellow CD solution. Additionally, we controlled effectively the degree of PL enhancement of CDs by adjusting the feed ratio of colloidal Au and CDs. In this case, it is highlighted that the GCDs exhibit the high PL QY of 39%. Furthermore, GCDs film with a bright white fluorescence under blue LED light were scored with CIE of (0.33, 0.36) and CRI of 78.2. And hence, we explored white LED based on the GCDs, providing a potentiality and insight into new generation of environment and friendly optoelectronic devices.

All the chemicals in the experiments were of analytical grade and were used without any further purification. Ethyl alcohol, anhydrous citric acid, N-(-aminoethyl)-g-aminopropyl methldimethoxy silane (AEAPMS), tetrachloroauric acid trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), sodium citrate tribasic dihydrate (Na3C6H5O7·2H2O) and all other chemicals were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Water for reaction and analysis was deionized using a Milli-Q water purification system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the colloidal Au NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Colloidal Au NPs were fabricated through a citrate-mediated reduction of HAuCl4 according to Frens's method [28]. 2.0 mL of 1% Na3C6H5O7·2H2O was rapidly injected into the boiling mixture made up of 1 mL of boiling 1% HAuCl4 and 25 mL Milli-Q water. The mixture was refluxed for 15 min and then cooled down to room temperature. A wine red colloids was obtained, indicating the formation of the colloidal Au NPs. The resulting gold colloids were stored in brown glass bottle at 4 °C.

CDs were prepared as similar protocol [29,30]. Briefly, 40 mL of AEAPMS were added into a three-necked flask and then heated under a nitrogen atmosphere. Afterward, 2.0 g of citric acid was dispersed absolutely in 4 mL of ethanol. The precipitate was quickly injected into the flask under robust stirring when the temperature up to 220 °C. The mixture was kept at that temperature for 3 min. The initial solution changed from colorless to yellow and then finally to deep yellow, demonstrating the formation of CDs.

GCDs were synthesized by a facile solution cast method. Typically, 0.6, 1.2 and 2.4 mL of Au colloids were immersed in 5 mL of CDs, individually. The resultant was shaken for 30 min at room temperature, producing the GCDs solutions. Here, the molar concentration of Au colloid is calculated to be 0.036 mg/mL. Different volume ratio of Au colloid to CDs were 10.71%, 19.35% and 32.43%, (hereafter denoted as GCDs 1, GCDs 2 and GCDs 3) respectively. The schematic synthesis for the fabrication of GCDs as shown in Fig. 1 .

A flexible spin-coating technology was adopted to deposit the high-performance GCDs (GCDs 2) solution on the upper surface of quartz glass substrate (10 × 10 mm2). The stiff glass films were acquired by stoving at 120 °C for 6 h. Afterward, the GCDs-based WLEDs were constructed by coupling the glass film with the blue GaN LED chips.

The XRD patterns of Au NPs and CDs were obtained using an XRD (Bruker D8 Advance) in the 2θ range of 10–80°. The TEM, HRTEM and Energy Dispersive X-ray Detector (EDX) images were taken on a JEOL JEM 2100 microscope at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) was also conducted. UV–vis absorption spectra at the wavelength range of 200–800 nm were performed on a Varian Cary 5 G UV-vis-NIR spectrophotometer. Observation of the functional groups of CDs and GCDs were carried out on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer. The XPS analyses were performed using an AXIS Ultra DLD spectrometer (Kratos Analytical Inc., Manchester, UK) with a monochromated Al Ka source at a power of 180 W (15 kV 12 mA). PL spectra and PL QYs were collected on a Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluromax-4P spectrophotometer. Time resolved PL lifetime measurements were recorded using a time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) lifetime spectroscopy system with a picosecond-pulsed diode laser (EPL-400 nm) as the single wavelength excitation light source. Optical properties of CRI, CIE, CCT, and LE were conducted employing an integrating sphere (PMS-50, Everfine, China) under an operating current of 20 mA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Au NPs, CDs and GCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 shows the XRD patterns of colloidal Au NPs and CDs. The standard pattern of Au (JCPDS no. 04-0784) is shown as a reference. The XRD pattern of Au NPs (Fig. 2) shows four sharp diffraction peaks assigned to the (111), (200), (220) and (311) planes, which reveals the high crystalline degree of the material. Additionally, the XRD pattern of CDs (Fig. 2, inset) exhibits a broad peak centered at around 2θ = 20°, further indicating the structure of an amorphous nature of CDs [30]. In order to further validate the morphology and crystallinity of the as-obtained Au NPs, CDs and GCDs under optimal synthesis conditions, the TEM and HRTEM images of them are presented in Fig. 3 . The TEM (Fig. 3a–c) and HRTEM images (Fig. 3d–f) depict that as-fabricated products exhibit a relatively homogeneous and well-dispersed spherical shape. The size of the spherical Au NPs is approximately 15.6 nm (Fig. 3a) while that of the CDs is 5 nm (Fig. 3b). However, as revealed by Fig. 3c, the average size of GCDs is found to be 39.7 nm, which is much larger than that of the CDs (5.4 nm). The average size similar to CDs and GCDs reported by previous works [31]. The TEM (Fig. 3c) observation on the morphology of CDs in GCDs composites is a little difficult due to the high contrast of Au NPs and the low contrast of and CDs to the carbon-coated grid. CDs are adsorbed on the surface of Au NPs by conjugated via AEAPMS rather than monodisperse, certifing metal-space-fluorophore systems to enhance the fluorescence of CDs [26]. Meanwhile, such an observation suggests that the GCDs produced tend to aggregate into “clusters” because of the affinity of the surface functional groups and the hydrogen bonds in the solution [32]. In order to investigate the structure of GCDs, the Energy Dispersive X-ray Detector (EDX) spectrum of GCDs is described in Fig. S1 (supporting information). EDX chemical elements analysis of the observed GCDs implies that dark spherical dots consist of element C, O, Si, and Au. Besides, combining with the HRTEM images, the clear lattice spacing (2.35 Å) of the NPs is corresponding to the (111) lattice plane of Au NPs. Meanwhile, the lattice spacing of the smaller regions agrees well with the (002) plane of the crystalline graphite. As we analyzed before, it is a core shell structure that the CDs are adsorbed on the surface of Au NPs.

To further ensure the combination of Au and CDs, the energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) was conducted. As revealed in Fig. 4 , blue, red, rose red and green spots symbolize Au, C, Si and N. It is clear that blue spots are adsorbed on the surface of red spots which indicates that CDs are attached to the surface of Au NPs. It is consistent well with the TEM.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of the as-synthesized GCDs, CDs and colloidal Au NPs were investigated at room temperature. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the characteristic absorption peaks located at 260 nm and 360 nm denote the sp2 hybridized structure and the sp3 hybridized structure, which result from the CC and CC bonds and the n–π* transition in the CO bond, respectively [33,34]. The sp2 clusters affect the energy gap between the π–π* states and can lead to a broad PL emission. The colloidal Au NPs present a broad peak at 520 nm, which indicates the presence of Au NPs in nano-scale by surface plasmon effect [35–37]. Evidently, there is no obvious absorption at around 520 nm can be observed in GCDs' UV–vis absorption spectra. But the plasmon resonance absorption peak of Au in the GCDs occurred at 520 nm when we amplified the region of the UV–vis spectrum of GCDs from 360 to 530 nm (Fig. 5b). On the one hand, due to the low concentration (0.036 mg/mL) of Au NPs in the CDs solution, the peak assigned to Au NPs is so weak that absorption spectra is unconspicuous. On the other hand, it may be assigned to the electron charge transfer process from AEAPMS to the CDs [26,38].

The FTIR spectra of as-obtained samples was acquired to identify the variation of the surface functional groups presented in Fig. 6 . The spectra of samples show obvious stretching vibrations bands of OH at 3704 and 3076 cm−1, NH at 3544 and 3214 cm−1, CH at 2988 and 2793 cm−1. Moreover, the peaks at 1450, 1070-1098 and 790 cm−1 corresponding to the signals of the CH2, SiOSi and SiCH2 bonds appear in all spectra. Obviously, the peak intensity of the OH, NH and CO stretching group increased due to further modification of CDs. The band centered at 1740 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the CO bonds in the COO group, which endows the CDs and GCDs with the excellent water solubility so that Au NPs solution can be mutual solubilized with CDs. Meanwhile, the procedures of AEAPMS copped CDs were exhibited [30]. The synthesis of CDs includes the decomposition and pyrolysis of anhydrous citric acid, coupled with the surface passivation reaction of the carboxyl groups driven from the pyrolyzed species with the amine groups of AEAPMS.

The valence states of the elements in carbon materials were determined by XPS. Clearly, C, O, Si, and N elements on the surface of CDs are displayed in Fig. 7 a. In addition, deconvolutions of the C1s spectrum (Fig. 7b) exhibit the presence of three types of carbon bonds: sp2 CC (284.3 eV), CN (285.2 eV) and CO (288.14 eV), which are consistent with FTIR spectra [29,30]. Fig. 7c shows the spectrum of O1s, which includes three peaks located around at 529 eV, 531 eV and 533 eV corresponding to CO, CO and COH, respectively [30]. Fig. 7d shows the spectrum of Si 2p, the peaks located indicating the groups of SiOSi. The as-synthesized GCDs was also investigated. Fig. 8 a shows high-resolution XPS of Au 4f, C 1s, O1s and Si 2p. The Au 4f spectrum (Fig. 8a) of the GCDs exhibits two peaks centered at 83.5 and 87.1 eV indicating the metallic Auo feature [31]. Overall, the plentiful oxygen-containing groups also reveal that the CDs possess excellent water-solubility, which was consistent well with the FTIR spectra. On account of the above analysis, the CDs mainly are consisted of sp2 graphitic carbons with some sp3 carbon defects and abundant hydroxy, aromatic rings, alkoxy and carboxylate/carboxyl groups on their surfaces. Therefore, the quantum size effect and surface state of CDs are the reasons why the PL of CDs was formed [6,39], wherein Au NPs serve as a fluorescent multiplier to enhance the photoluminescence intensity of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL proprieties and mechanisms of the GCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9 a shows the fluorescent emission spectra of a series of CDs and GCDs 1, GCDs 2 and GCDs 3 samples (centered at 575 nm) under 460 nm excitation. With the addition of colloidal Au NPs, the fluorescence intensity initially increases significantly, and then decreases dramatically with increasing a lesser amount. Varying the feed ratio of colloidal Au NPs and AEAPMS-modified the CDs can affect the fluorescence enhancement factors. The PL enhancement of GCDs 2 is about 3 times larger than the pristine CDs, as shown in Fig. 9b. Meanwhile, the Au NPs also possess the fluorescent properties, as exhibited in Fig. S2 (supporting information). Comparing with the PL spectra of GCDs and CDs under 347 nm excitation, the intensity of Au peak is relatively weaker. Moreover, the PL enhancement factor is up to 1 fold by comparing the intensity of GCDs and CDs. Thus, the fluorescence from GCDs is the result of their interaction rather than a simple superposition of them. In order to further explore the PL properties of GCDs, PL of the obtained GCDs 2 (centered at 575 nm) was studied using different excitation wavelengths, as illustrated in Fig. 9c. Unlike most luminescent CDs, the GCDs exhibits an excitation-wavelength-independent PL characteristic. The homogeneity for the size of carbon fluorophore is very important to determine an excitation-wavelength-independent emission behavior. As we all know, MEF is increasingly becoming prevalent among the theories of fluorescence enhancement with metallic surfaces and particles (Au NPs), as depicted in Fig. 9d [40,41]. Currently, MEF is considered to be comprised of two synergetic mechanisms and near-field interactions: (1) an electric field effect and (2) an induced plasmon effect [42]. Essentially, a fluorophore through-space induced mirror dipole in the metal and the surface plasmons in turn, then radiate the emission (quanta) of the coupling fluorophore, called surface plasmon coupled fluorescence (SPCF). Surface plasmons are collective oscillations of free electrons in a metal at an interface with a dielectric [43,44]. An enhancement of the local electromagnetic fields occurred by resonance of excitation of surface plasmons [45,46]. In addition, Au NPs enable the storage of the incident energy in localized surface plasmon modes [46].

In this study, the fluorescent enhancement of the CDs is well desired because Au NPs and CDs keep suitable distance apart due to the linked AEAPMS. It is worth noting that metal-space-fluorophore systems (MSFS) could interpret enhancing the fluorescence of fluorophores [26]. In the electric field effect, CDs fluorophores proximity (<10 nm) to Au NPs are exposed to the increased electric fields in between and around Au NPs, effectively resulting in significant heighten in their absorption cross section, as shown in the UV–vis absorption spectra of the GCDs and CDs [19]. Au NPs can strengthen the photon energy in small region, which significantly enhances the local electromagnetic field [43]. Subsequently, the effect increase the excitation and eventually strength the fluorescence emission of the CDs fluorophores. In the induced plasmon effect, the excited state energies of CDs fluorophores are partially transferred to Au NPs surface plasmons (induced surface plasmons), bringing about increasing of the PL.

Fig. 10 shows the fluorescent decay behaviors of CDs and GCDs under 462 nm excitation. The PL decay curves are delivered by fitting the traces with a biexponential function:
(1)
Y
( t )
=
α 1
exp
( − τ /
t 1
)
+
α 2
exp
( − τ /
t 2
)
where α1 and α2 are the fractional contributions of decay lifetimes τ1 and τ2. The average lifetime can be concluded using the following relation:
(2)
τ
a v
=
α 1
τ 1 2
+
α 2
τ 2 2
α 1
τ 1
+
α 2
τ 2

The emission decay of all the samples is consisted of two processes: a short-lived component τ1 and a long-lived component τ2, which are related to the electronic transitions in the CDs [47,48]. With compared to the sheer CDs, the average PL lifetimes of GCDs were reduced to 11.36 ns as summarized in Table 1 of the fitting parameters. In the electric field effect, CDs fluorophores in close to Au NPs are exposed to the increased electric fields, effectively resulting in significant increases in their absorption cross section. Subsequently, the PL of the CDs was increased, noting that the fluorescence lifetime remains nearly unchanged [41]. The synergistic processes are related to the electronic transitions in the CDs, indicating the coupling among the CDs core and surface states. Meanwhile, the surfaces interactions can suppresses non-radiative decay processes [49]. In addition, the enhancement of the fluorescence QY up to 39.9% was observed under excitation at 462 nm. When the concentration of Au NPs is low, the electric field in the vicinity of a Au NPs is dramatically amplified, which is useful for exciting a nearby CDs emitter. When is high, the distance become small. The smaller the distance, the stronger the near electric field is. Once the CDs was excited, it behaves as an electric dipole, and the Forster resonance energy transfer between the dipole and the Au NPs is unavoidable if the distance is very small. Particularly when the gap is too small, the nonradiative part dominates the CDs's emission, so a decreased QY is observed [42,50,51]. Therefore, it is meaningful to achieve a relative enhancement in QY in comparison with previous works [6–9].

The as-prepared GCDs 2 films S3 show the excellent fluorescence, which have potential practical applications in the solid-state lighting and displays [7–11]. Therefore, we employed GCDs film in white LED lighting application. As a proof-of-concept experiment, the fabricated white LED device is shown in Fig. 11 c, which consist of the blue GaN-based chip and GCDs 2 film with different thickness (0.3, 0.5, 0.7 and 0.8 mm). As manifested by Fig. 11a, the as-prepared white LED with varying GCDs 2 film thickness display the CIE of (0.24, 0.22), (0.28, 0.32), (0.33, 0.36) and (0.42, 0.49), which are close to that of balanced white-light emission (0.33, 0.33). The electroluminescent spectrum of the white LED was also shown in Fig. 11b, which consists of two emission bands: a blue emission centered at 460 nm from the blue GaN-based chip and a broad yellow emission from GCDs 2 film S3. The two emission bands are mixed to produce warm white light. The measured optical parameters of the GCDs 2 film at various thickness are listed in Table 2 . Obviously, the LE of GCDs 2 film increase and then decrease with the increasing film thickness, which is consistent with the PL QY measurements. It is interesting that the optimum CRI is 78.2, which is comparable to the commercial white LED. More importantly, the stability of the fabricated white LED was examined during operation over 5 h and no changes appeared in the emission spectrum. These results demonstrate that white LED are successfully fabricated based on the yellow emission of the GCDs film. Although a few LED applications have been demonstrated recently, most of them employed the low quantum yield of CDs and white LED with cool withe light [6–9]. The GCDs 2 film S3 as a solid matrix have many advantages such as improved fluorescence QYs and average lifetimes, which can provide flexibility and enhance physical stability. Thus, such a film may have several advantages over a solid array of CDs or their powdered form. Therefore, the solid-state monolithic GCDs film could have promising applications like phosphors and perovskite in the next generation of LEDs [52,53].

In summary, a controllable enhancement of PL intensity of yellow-emitting CDs was obtained by incorporating with Au NPs through a simpler and facile method. Varying the feed ratio of colloidal Au NPs and CDs can affect effectively the fluorescence enhancement of GCDs. The PL intensity of CDs can be strengthened as much as 3 fold. Particularly, according to the previous researches, we have explored the MEF mechanism of GCDs hybrids that may be comprised of Au NPs and CDs cooperatives mechanisms and near-field interactions. Besides, GCDs film was realized combining GCDs with quartz glass through a spin-coating technology. With simply regulating the thickness of GCDs film, the resulting white LED exhibits excellent performances with an optimal CIE of (0.33, 0.36), CRI of 78.2 and CCT of 5653 K. Therefore, such excellent optical properties of GCDs films give insight into the search for novel carbon-based white LED devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was financially supported by the Zhejiang Province Natural Science Foundation (LQ14E020006) and National Nature Science Foundation of China (51472183 and 51672192).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2017.01.043.
